segments.s098-g9: ز نیروی او پشت کردی به خم is a compact, poetic clause. ز + نیروی او = “by/through his strength” (ز as a poetic preposition). پشت کردی is verb + object with کردی the 2nd-person past of کردن (you did/made); here کردی takes پشت as its object and means “you bent/caused the back (to be)”; به خم is a prepositional phrase (‘into/unto a bend’) modifying how you bent the back.
segments.s098-g10: نهادی به روی زمین بر شکم uses نهادی in its finite past form (2nd-person singular) of نهادن “to place/put” (you laid/placed). به روی زمین = “onto/on the ground” (به + روی), and بر شکم = “on (his) belly” with بر indicating position upon the stomach. The two prepositional phrases describe where you placed him after bending his back.
segments.s098-g11: چنین تا ز کابل بیامد زرنگ: چنین تا = “thus/until” setting a continuing scene; ز کابل بیامد = “came from Kabul” (بیامد is the simple past ‘came’); زرنگ follows and can be read as a name or an adjectival epithet ‘the clever one’ — here it functions as the subject that arrived from Kabul.
segments.s098-g12: فسیله همی تاخت از رنگ‌رنگ: فسیله is the subject (likely a horse’s name or a noun), همی + تاخت is an iterative past—همی before a past verb gives a continuous/ongoing sense so همی تاخت = “kept rushing/galloping.” از رنگ‑رنگ is a descriptive postmodifier meaning “of varied/multicolored appearance,” with از marking the source/characteristic.
segments.s098-g13: یکی مادیان تیز بگذشت خنگ: یکی introduces a single instance “one” or “a certain”; مادیان تیز = “a swift mare” (noun + adjective, adjective after noun in Persian poetry is common); بگذشت is the simple past ‘passed by’ (3rd-person singular); خنگ follows as a descriptive adjective applied to that mare, meaning ‘stupid’ or ‘dazed.’
segments.s098-g14: برش چون بر شیر و کوتاه لنگ: چون = “like/as” introduces a simile; برش is used to indicate her aspect/appearance or bearing (here linked to the simile) and is followed by بر شیر = “like a lion” (بر = on/like, شیر = lion). و کوتاه لنگ is a coordinate descriptive phrase “and short, lame” (two adjectives modifying the mare).
segments.s098-g15: دو گوشش چو دو خنجر آبدار: دو گوشش = “his two ears” (دو + noun + possessive ـش); چو دو خنجر = “like two daggers” (چو = like); آبدار is an adjectival epithet attached to خنجر here, giving a vivid quality to the daggers (poetic adjective). The simile compares the ears directly to daggers.
segments.s098-g16: بر و یال فربه میانش نزار: this line lists body parts and contrasts their conditions. بر و یال = “(the) fore (quarters) and mane” (بر here names a fore/upper part in context), فربه qualifies them as “fat/robust.” میانش = ‘his middle’ (possessive suffix on میان ‘middle’), and نزار is an adjective meaning “emaciated/lean.” The word order places contrasted descriptors in parallel.
segments.s098-g41: بینداخت is a past-tense verb 'threw/cast' placed at the start (classical narrative often puts the verb before subject). رستم کیانی is the subject with کیانی an epithet attached to the name; کمند is the direct object 'lasso/snare' placed after the subject.
segments.s098-g42: This chunk contains a small clause: 'سر ابرش' is a noun phrase (سر = 'head') with the possessive enclitic -ش on ابر; آورد is the past 'brought/caused to come'; ناگه is an adverb 'suddenly'; ببند (here narrative/resultive) means 'shut/fasten, bind'—the present-stem form is used in classical poetry to report a quick action. Word order: object phrase → verb → adverb → verb-form indicating the quick action.
segments.s098-g43: بیامد is the past 'came' and appears before its subject (typical in poetic narrative). چو introduces a simile 'like/as'; شیر ژیان is the simile's noun phrase ('a living/ferocious lion'), and مادرش is the actual subject 'his mother' with the possessive enclitic -ش.
segments.s098-g44: همی + خواست is a classical/perfective-progressive construction: همی marks an ongoing or imminent action and خواست is 'wanted/tried' (past). کندن is the infinitive 'to pull off/tear away' and follows خواست to express attempted action. به دندان specifies the instrument ('with the teeth'), and سرش is the object 'his head' with the possessive enclitic.
segments.s098-g45: بغرید is a past tense verb 'roared/bellowed' placed first for narrative force. رستم is the subject; چو + شیر ژیان is a simile 'like a living/ferocious lion' modifying the manner of the roar.
segments.s098-g46: از آواز او is a prepositional phrase 'from/because of his voice' indicating cause; خیره شد is a two-word predicate 'became astonished/stunned' (شد = 'became'), and مادیان (placed at the end) is the subject 'the mare'. The construction marks cause → result.
segments.s098-g47: یکی is an indefinite 'one/someone' serving as subject; مشت زد is the verb+object sequence 'struck a blow' (زد = struck); نیز is the adverb 'also/likewise'; بر گردنش is a prepositional phrase 'upon his neck' with the possessive enclitic -ش. Word order places the verb-object chunk before the locative phrase.
segments.s098-g48: کزان is a relative/causal particle 'from which/therefrom' referring back to that blow; مشت برگشت means 'the blow rebounded/caused a reaction'; لرزان is an adjective 'trembling' modifying تنش ('his body' with enclitic -ش). The phrase describes the result of the blow: his body shaking/recoiling.
segments.s098-g25: کمند کیانی is a noun phrase meaning “the royal lasso” (کمند + adjectival/attributive کیانی). همی داد is a classical/perfective past sequence where همی gives a continuous or emphatic flavor before the past verb داد (“gave/threw”); خم is a noun “a bend/curve” that here describes the action applied to the lasso (literally “gave the lasso a bend” = cast it into a loop). Word order is poetic: object/phrase + verbal phrase.
segments.s098-g26: که introduces a subordinate (purpose/result) clause “so that / in order that.” آن کره را is the definite object phrase “that foal” (آن = that, کره = calf/foal, را object marker). بازگیرد is a 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive form meaning “may take back / retrieve.” ز is the poetic preposition “from,” and رم means “flight/stampede.”
segments.s098-g27: به رستم is the indirect object/dative “to Rostam.” چنین is an adverb “thus/so.” گفت is the past-tense verb “said/spoke” (here used with verb–subject inversion typical in poetry), and چوپان پیر is the subject “the old shepherd.” The line reads verb + indirect object + adverb + subject in poetic order: “to Rostam thus said the old shepherd.”
segments.s098-g28: که‌ای is a vocative/exclamatory address (خَیه/که ای) equivalent to “O” or “O you.” مهتر is the addressee title “chief/elder.” اسپ کسان را is the object phrase “the horses of the people” with را marking the definite object (کسان = those/people, here “the people’s”). مگیر is the negative imperative/presentive form “do not take/seize.”
segments.s098-g29: بپرسید is a past 3rd‑person singular verb “asked” (here placed before the subject in poetic word order). رستم is the subject. که introduces the content-question clause; این اسپ کیست is the interrogative “this horse, who is (it)?” meaning “who owns this horse?” — کیست is the copula + interrogative “who is.”
segments.s098-g30: که continues the subordinate/explanatory clause. دو رانش (literally “its two flanks/thighs”) is the grammatical subject phrase. از introduces the source/measure “from/of,” داغ آتش means “the heat/fire’s burn” (داغ = heat/brand, آتش = fire), and تهیست is a predicative verb “is empty/devoid (of).” Together: “for its two flanks are devoid of the heat of fire.”
segments.s098-g31: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed poetic phrase “thus gave answer” (داد = gave, پاسخ = answer). که introduces the reported content. داغش is داغ + enclitic pronoun ش “its mark/brand/scorch,” and مجوی is the negative imperative/subjunctive “do not seek/look for.” So: “Thus he answered, ‘Do not seek its mark...’”.
segments.s098-g32: کزین = از + این (poetic fused form) “from this / because of this.” هست is the copula “is.” هر گونه‌ای گفت‌وگوی = “every sort/kind of conversation/talk” (هر = every, گونه‌ای = sort/kind, گفت‌وگوی = conversation/talk). The clause gives the reason: “for from this is every kind of talk.”
segments.s098-g1: چنان شد is a fixed phrase meaning “became thus” (چنان = so/thus placed before the past verb شد). ز here is the old/poetic preposition meaning “because of / from,” linking گفتار او (“his speech/utterance”) as the cause; پهلوان is the predicate noun (“hero, champion”) placed after the causal phrase. Word order is typical of epic Persian: adverb + past verb + causal preposition + noun phrase + predicate.
segments.s098-g2: که introduces a result/subordinate clause (“that …”). گفتی is used as a noun (“words / speech / the saying”), not as a verbal form here. برافشاند is the verb “scatter / throw away”; خواهد is the future auxiliary (‘will’) placed before the rest of the predicate (a typical classical ordering), and روان is the object meaning “soul/spirit.” The clause therefore states a consequence: “that the words will scatter the spirit.”
segments.s098-g3: گله is “herd/flock” (not ‘complaint’ here); هرچ is an indefinite modifier “whatever / however many”; بودش is the past form with enclitic -ش indicating third person (“there was to him / he had” or simply “he had (there)”), and به زابلستان is the locative/directional phrase “in/to Zabulistan.” The phrase says which herds are meant (those he had in Zabulistan).
segments.s098-g4: بیاورد is the past tense “he brought / he took”; لختی is a temporal adverb “for a short while”; به کابلستان is the destination “to Kabulistan.” The verb comes first (classical epic ordering) and the adverb and destination follow, giving the meaning “brought … for a while to Kabulistan.”
segments.s098-g5: همه is the subject “all / everyone.” پیش رستم is a prepositional phrase “before Rostam / in Rostam’s presence.” همی is the old continuous/stative particle marking repeated or ongoing action with past tense, and راندند is the past plural “they drove” (here: they drove the herds or drove them forward). The clause thus describes a repeated action performed in front of Rostam.
segments.s098-g6: برو dاغ شاهان is the content of what they chanted: داغ here means “grief / dirge / mourning,” with شاهان “of the kings” in genitive. همی خواندند is again the continuous past “they kept reciting/singing.” Put together: they repeatedly sang the kings’ lament. (برو here appears as the vocative/exclamatory element attached to the lament in the poem’s diction.)
segments.s098-g7: هر اسپی is “each/ every horse.” The relative clause که رستم کشیدیش is “that Rostam had drawn/pulled (forward).” کشیدیش is an older/poetic past form of کشیدن with enclitic marking; here it denotes a completed action by Rostam. پیش functions as “forward / before him” (indicating the horses were brought forward).
segments.s098-g8: به پشتش literally “onto its/his back” (پشت + enclitic -ش = “its/his back”); بیفشاردی is a poetic imperfective/past-habitual form meaning “he would press / he laid (his hand)” (here indicating a repeated or customary action). دست خویش is “his own hand” (خویش as reflexive/own, followed again by the enclitic -ش); word order places the prepositional target first and the agent’s instrument afterward, as is common in Ferdowsi’s diction.
segments.s098-g49: A sequence of three past actions: بیفتاد (past 3rd sg) 'he fell', followed by the conjunction و 'and', then برخاست 'he rose/stood up', another و, and برگشت 'he turned/returned'. The prepositional phrase از وی 'from him' follows برگشت and marks the source/point he turned away from.
segments.s098-g50: بسوی introduces a goal 'toward', followed by گله 'herd' as the goal. تیز is an adverb/adjective 'swift/keen' modifying the motion, and بنهاد (archaic past of نهادن) is used here as a simple past 'he set/placed' (i.e., 'set his face'). روی 'face' is the noun object of بنهاد in the idiom 'روی بنهاد' = 'set (one's) face toward'.
segments.s098-g51: The past verb بیفشارد (he pressed/squeezed) appears first, followed by its object ران 'thigh'. The subject follows in apposition: رستم زورمند 'Rostam the mighty' (typical poetic word order: V + object + subject + epithet).
segments.s098-g52: کرد is the past auxiliary/lexical verb 'made/did'. تنگتر is the comparative 'tighter' (adverbial here: made tighter). خم کمند 'the bend/curve of the lasso' is the direct object. برو appears adverbially/particle-like here (from برو 'go') functioning to intensify or mark the onward action — in context it helps convey 'further / then' as the tightening action is performed.
segments.s098-g53: بیازید is an archaic/poetic past-form meaning 'it opened/gaped' or 'it burst forth'; here it governs the following noun phrase. چنگال 'claw(s)' is the noun affected, گردی 'dust' (a poetic collocation: the claw(s) raising dust) and بزور 'by force' is an adverbial phrase describing how the action happened.
segments.s098-g54: بیفشارد again is past 'he pressed/squeezed'. یک دست 'one hand' is the instrument, بر 'on/upon' is the preposition, پشت 'back' is the location, and بور 'the fair/pale (one)' describes whose back it is (an epithet for the animal/person). Word order: verb + instrument + prepositional phrase marking where the hand was placed.
segments.s098-g55: نکرد is the negative past 'did not do'. ایچ is an emphatic negative 'at all/nothing'. پشت 'the back' is the patient/affected part, از فشردن is a gerundial/ablative phrase 'from/by pressing', and تهی 'empty' is the result-state predicated of پشت. Together: 'he did not, by pressing, leave the back empty at all.'
segments.s098-g56: تو 'you' is the 2nd-person subject. گفتی here functions as 'you said' (quoted or reported speech). ندارد 'has not' negates the following noun آگهی 'information/news'; همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('at all/indeed') that intensifies the negation. The clause reads as reported speech: 'you said (that) there is no news.'
segments.s098-g17: یکی کره = 'one foal' (indefinite numeral + noun) is the nominal subject. از پس به بالای او is a sequence of prepositional/adverbial elements: از پس (literally 'from behind') + به بالای او ('onto/at the top of her' — بالای used as 'back, atop') describing the foal's position relative to the mare (او = 'her' referring to the mare mentioned earlier). Word order is typical Persian: numeral + noun, then location phrases.
segments.s098-g18: سرین ('saddle') names an object placed on the foal; و برش هم coordinates another feature (برش here refers to a part/profile/side of the animal). هم is an emphatic/additive particle 'also/too'. به پهنای او is a comparative phrasing: به + پهنای او = 'to the width of her', i.e. 'as wide as she (is)'. The whole segment links to the first by listing what the foal carried and its dimensions relative to the mare.
segments.s098-g19: This is a string of adjectival descriptions coordinated by و. سیه چشم = 'black‑eyed' (adjective before noun as in classical Persian). بورابرش is a compound describing color/marking of the flank/side (an older poetic compound; here it refers to a dun/brown marking on the body). گاودم literally 'cow‑tail' (= a long tail resembling a cow's) is another attribute. The nouns/adjectives are juxtaposed without copula, a normal epic style.
segments.s098-g20: Another coordinated run of adjectives/nouns: سیه خایه = 'black testicles' (سیه modifies خایه), و تند = 'and swift/fast' (تند = quick), و پولادسم is a compound adjective 'poulad‑سم' = 'iron‑hoofed' (پولاد 'iron' + سم 'hoof'). Again no overt verb; these are qualities enumerated of the animal.
segments.s098-g21: تنش = 'its body' (تن + enclitic possessive ش = 'his/its'). پرنگار is an adjective 'richly ornamented, highly figured'. از کران تا کران is an idiomatic locative span 'from edge to edge' = 'all over'. The clause states a quality of the body; verb 'was' is omitted (copula), typical of Persian descriptive lines.
segments.s098-g22: چو = 'like/as' introduces a simile. داغ here is used in the sense 'mark/spot' (a stain or conspicuous mark), گل سرخ = 'red flower' (literally 'rose' or 'red flower'), بر = 'on/upon', زعفران in this poetic context denotes the saffron (i.e. saffron‑colored ground of the coat). Together: 'like the mark of a red flower on saffron' — a visual comparison for the ornamentation.
segments.s098-g23: چو = 'when'. رستم is the subject. بران (an older/deictic form) here functions as 'at that'/'upon that' and links to مادیان (the mare): 'when Rostam looked at that mare'. بنگرید is the past/epic-form of 'to look'/'he looked' (classical epic diction). Word order places the temporal particle first, then subject and the locative phrase, finishing with the verb.
segments.s098-g24: مر is an archaic/dialectal clitic (etymologically 'me'/'to me') that often appears in classical Persian and can function as a discourse particle here; the clause reads naturally without translating it literally. آن کره پیلتن را = 'that foal of Piltan' (آن + noun + proper/place name + را object marker). بدید is the past of دیدن 'to see' ('he saw'). Together: '(then) he saw that Piltan foal.'
segments.s098-g73: چنان گشت is a past-state expression: چنان (so/thus) + گشت (became) means 'became so'. ابرش is the noun ابر (cloud) with the enclitic -ش 'his/its'. که here introduces the result/clause 'that/so that'. هر شب is a time phrase 'every night' placed before the following noun. سپند is the object of the next clause (harmal used as incense).
segments.s098-g74: همی is the old/poetic imperfective marker here meaning 'continually/would' or 'kept on'. سوختندش is the verbal form (they burned it) with the object clitic -ش; the verb is active 'they burned'. ز introduces an ablative/causal reason 'because of/for', and بیم گزند is the phrase 'fear of harm' (بیم 'fear', گزند 'harm').
segments.s098-g75: چپ و راست literally 'left and right' is an idiomatic adverbial 'everywhere/left and right'. گفتی here is a noun (speech/rumor) or a substantival use meaning 'talk/rumor' (not the 2nd-person verb), so چپ و راست گفتی = 'talk everywhere'. که introduces reported content 'that'. جادو شدست is a perfect/result phrase: جادو 'magic' + شدست (archaic compound of شدن) 'has been made/is become', meaning 'has been enchanted'.
segments.s098-g76: به آورد here is the verb آوردن with the prepositional/constitutive particle به preceding it; together in this context it means 'brought about/caused' or 'was brought (to be)'. تا is used resultively 'so that/in such a way that' (not temporal). زنده آهو شدست is again the result/perfective phrase: زنده آهو 'a living gazelle' + شدست 'it became/was made', meaning 'it had been turned into a living gazelle'.
segments.s098-g77: دل زال is the construct 'the heart of Zal' (دل + proper name زال). زر شد is a simple past/result 'became gold' (زر 'gold' + شد 'became'). چو here is the simile conjunction 'like/as', and خرم بهار is the noun phrase 'the joyful/lush spring' acting as the basis of comparison.
segments.s098-g78: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing the cause or source. رخش is 'steed' (a horse name in epic often), نوآیین is an adjective 'newly adorned/new-fashioned', and the phrase و فرخ سوار links another descriptor 'and the fortunate/happy rider' (فرخ 'fortunate' modifying سوار 'rider/horseman'). Word order places the modifying adjectives after the nouns, typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s098-g79: در گنج بگشاد is a verbal clause: در گنج 'in/into the treasure (chest)' + بگشاد, an archaic past form of گشودن 'he opened' (the prefixed ب- is the classical past marker). و دینار داد joins the coordinated action 'and gave dinars' (دینار 'coin/money'; داد is the past tense 'gave').
segments.s098-g80: از امروز و فردا is an adverbial phrase 'from today and tomorrow' meaning 'from then on' or 'henceforth'. نیامدش is the negative past/impersonal 'did not come to him' (the object clitic -ش refers back to یاد), and یاد 'memory/remembrance' is the subject/experiencer that 'did not occur' to him (i.e., he did not remember).
segments.s098-g65: لب رستم از خنده شد چون بسد is a compact classical clause: لب رستم (possessive + noun) = “Rostam’s lip.” از خنده is a source phrase (از + noun) = “from/out of laughter.” شد is the past/preterite copula ‘became’ (literary use). چون here is a temporal/aspectual particle (classical/poetic) meaning ‘when/as,’ and بسد (classical form of بسیدن/بسیدن or به‌سدن) is used in the sense ‘be enough / subside’ — so the phrase means his lip was affected by laughter and when that laughter was enough/subsided. Word order is noun–modifier (لب رستم) then circumstantial phrase (از خنده) then verb (شد) then temporal clause (چون بسد).
segments.s098-g66: همی گفت نیکی ز یزدان سزد: همی is the frequentative/adverbial ‘kept / continually’ (classical). گفت here functions as ‘he said’ (classical past) though the supplied sense list is nominal; treat it as the reported-speech verb. نیکی is the subject of the reported clause (‘goodness / good deed’). ز is the poetic preposition ‘from,’ linking نیکی to یزدان ‘God.’ سزد is an impersonal/present classical predicate meaning ‘is fitting / befits.’ Word order inside the quoted clause is predicate-final (nominative-like noun then prepositional source then verb), typical of classical Persian report clauses.
segments.s098-g67: به زین اندر آورد گلرنگ را is a trajective clause: به + زین + اندر آورد = ‘into the saddle he brought/mounted.’ اندر (classical) = ‘into/in,’ آورد = past of آوردن ‘to bring/put’ (here ‘to place in the saddle’). گلرنگ را (noun + را) is the definite/direct object marked by را; گلرنگ functions as a horse’s name or a descriptive name ‘the flower-colored one.’ The verb follows its directional phrase, a typical order in poetry.
segments.s098-g68: سرش تیز شد کینه و جنگ را: سرش = سر + enclitic possessive ش ‘his’ = ‘his head/spirits.’ تیز شد is verb phrase ‘became sharp/keen’ (شد = became). کینه و جنگ را are the objects/topics marked by را and express the things his head became keen for: ‘hatred and war.’ The را after جنگ marks the following noun phrase as definite/emphatic. The clause literally says his head became sharp for (the purposes of) hatred and battle (i.e., his mind/resolve was sharpened towards enmity and fighting).
segments.s098-g69: گشاده زنخ دیدش و تیزتگ: گشاده is an adjective ‘wide / open.’ زنخ here is a noun used of a facial/jaw feature or harsh expression (poetic/archaic); the adjective precedes the noun as usual. دیدش is دید + enclitic object ش = ‘he saw him/it.’ و links to the next nominal (تیزتگ). تیزتگ is a compound noun meaning ‘a small sharp blade / dagger’ (poetic). The sequence lists what was seen: an open/wide زنخ and a sharp dagger.
segments.s098-g70: بدیدش که دارد دل و تاو و رگ: بدیدش (past of دیدن with object clitic) = ‘he saw.’ که introduces a subordinate content clause ‘that…’. دارد is the present/habitual ‘has / possesses’ (classical narrative use in combination with past perception verbs). دل و تاو و رگ enumerate interior qualities: دل ‘heart’ (courage/feeling), تاو ‘radiance / glow’ (a poetic epithet), and رگ ‘vein’ (literally vein or figuratively lineage/tendency). The clause therefore reports his perception that the person possessed heart, radiance, and veins — i.e., vital/valorous qualities.
segments.s098-g71: کشد جوشن و خود و کوپال او: کشد is an archaic/poetic past form of کشیدن used here to mean ‘drew on / put on’ (i.e., to don armor) or more generally ‘took.’ جوشن is the armour/coat of mail (direct object). و خود ‘and his own’ marks possession/emphasis, and کوپال او names his weapon (Koopal) with او as the possessive clitic ‘his.’ The verb governs the following coordinated objects (armor and weapon); the sentence uses compact poetic coordination without repeating the verb.
segments.s098-g72: تن پیلوار و بر و یال او: تن = ‘body.’ پیلوار is a descriptive adjective/compound here meaning ‘in successive/layered fashion’ (poetic, describing how the body is clad). و بر و یال او lists other parts: بر (here functioning as ‘breast/chest’ in the poetic equipment-list) and یال او ‘his mane’ (literally mane, often used of helmet‑crest). The clause is a verbless descriptive continuation dependent on the previous verb (i.e., the donning/clothing applies to the body, breast and mane).
segments.s098-g57: بدل گفت کاین برنشست منست = literal word order: [بدل گفت] 'Badal/in return said' + subordinate clause. بدل here functions as an adverbial/response marker ('in return' / 'in reply') or a proper name in some contexts; گفت is the past of گفتن but the provided sense list lacks the simple 'said', so the clause introduces reported speech. کاین is the compound relative/demonstrative ('that/this which'); برنشست is a past tense verb formed of the preverb بر- + نشستن 'to sit' meaning 'sat upon/took the seat'; منست is an archaic contracted form (originally من است or from an adjective) giving a predicate 'is mine/appropriate to me' and closes the clause.
segments.s098-g58: کنون کار کردن به دست منست = 'now' (کنون) sets the temporal frame; کار کردن is a noun+verbal infinitive construction meaning 'the doing of the work/deed' (کار + کردن); به دست is an idiom 'in the hand/of the power of'; منست again closes the clause with the sense 'is (with) me / is mine / is appropriate to me'—poetically saying the task now lies with the speaker.
segments.s098-g59: ز چوپان بپرسید کاین اژدها = ز + چوپان marks the source/indirect object 'from the shepherd'; بپرسید is the past form 'he asked' (پرسیدن); کاین is the demonstrative 'this/that which' introducing the object of inquiry; اژدها is the noun 'dragon/monster'. The clause thus says he asked the shepherd concerning this dragon.
segments.s098-g60: به چندست و این را که خواهد بها = به چندست is a fixed interrogative phrase 'how much / what is it (worth)'; و simply links the next question; این را is the object/topic marker + demonstrative 'this (thing)'; که introduces a subordinate clause 'that (which)'; خواهد is the present/future auxiliary/lexical verb 'wants/will have' (here 'wants as a price'); بها is 'price/value'. Altogether: 'how much is it, and what price does he want for this?'.
segments.s098-g61: چنین داد پاسخ که‌گر رستمی = such an answer was given: چینـین + داد + پاسخ is the ordinary order 'gave such an answer'; که‌گر is the classical fused conditional 'که گر' = 'if'; رستمی (here) names or designates Rostam/the hero as the conditional subject. The segment introduces reported conditional speech.
segments.s098-g62: برو راست کن روی ایران زمی = imperatives instructing movement: برو 'go' is an imperative; راست کن is a compound imperative 'make (it) straight' (راست 'straight' + کن 'do'); روی here means 'face' (literally 'turn your face'); ایران زمی (زمی/زمینی) is an adjectival/poetic qualifier 'the land/earth of Iran' — so the instruction is 'go, make your course straight, turn your face toward the land of Iran.'
segments.s098-g63: مر این را بر و بوم ایران بهاست = مر is an archaic/dialectal form meaning 'to me' (مرا/برای من); این را is the demonstrative with the object/topic marker; بر و بوم is a fixed poetic pairing meaning 'land and dwelling / realm' (بر + بوم); ایران specifies whose land; بهاست (بها + است) states 'is the value/price' — together: 'To me, this is the value for the realm/soil of Iran.'
segments.s098-g64: بدین بر تو خواهی جهان کرد راست = بدین ('by/with this') opens an instrumental clause; the printed split (ب + دین) reflects the original orthography but functionally reads 'بدین' = 'with this'; بر تو = 'upon you' or 'for you'; خواهی is either present/future 'you will' or 'you desire' and here expresses future/result; جهان کرد راست is an idiomatic result 'make the world right/straight' (کرد 'make/do' + راست 'right/straight'). The clause says 'by this, you will set the world right.'
segments.s098-g33: همی before the verb gives a continuous/habitual sense ('we keep/always'); رخش is the name (here the direct object/name given); خوانیم is 1st person plural present (classical Persian) meaning 'we call/name' rather than 'read'; بورابرش است is a predicate: بورابرش ('equal, his match') + copula است, stating that (he) is the equal/counterpart. Word order places the habitual verb phrase before the descriptive predicate.
segments.s098-g34: This is a twofold predication joined by و: به خو آتشی = literally 'in (his) disposition fiery' (به + خو + adjectival آتشی) — به marks the adverbial/qualifying relation; آتشی is adjectival ('fiery'). Then به رنگ آتش است repeats the copular description: 'he is of the color of fire.' The final است is the copula tying both qualities to the steed.
segments.s098-g35: خداوند placed first gives an exclusive/contrastive sense ('God (alone) ...'); این را is the topicalized object (را marks definiteness/object); ندانیم is present negative (first person plural) 'we do not know'; کس here in the negative context functions as 'anyone/no one' — the clause as a whole expresses that only God knows this.
segments.s098-g36: همی again signals habitual action ('we call'); رخش is the object/name; رستمش is Rustom + enclitic -ش 'his/of him' forming 'Rostam's' (i.e. 'the one of Rostam'); خوانیم is 'we call' as before; و بس (and بس) is a fixed closing remark meaning 'and that is enough' or 'that's all.' The clause states a conventional naming and closes the remark.
segments.s098-g37: سه سالست is an archaic copular temporal phrase ('it is three years' / 'three years have passed'); تا here functions in the temporal sense 'since/that' introducing what has happened in that time; این بزین آمدست literally 'this (bazin / saddle/equipment) has come' — آمدست is the archaic perfect/copular form (آمد + -ست) meaning 'has come/arrived.' The clause says how long ago the saddle arrived.
segments.s098-g38: به چشم بزرگان is a prepositional phrase 'before the eyes of the great/elders' (به marks the relation, چشم = eyes, بزرگان = the great); گزین آمدست is a compound: گزین (chosen/selected) + آمدست ('came/appeared') — i.e. 'it was presented and chosen' (poetic compressed order: selection is predicated of its appearance to the elders).
segments.s098-g39: چو is the temporal/subordinating 'when'; مادرش is 'his mother' (مادر + enclitic -ش); بیند is the present tense 'sees' (classical form); کمند سوار is a compound noun phrase 'lasso-bearing/with lasso, mounted' (کمند = lasso, سوار = rider/mounted), the object seen. The clause sets a temporal circumstance for what follows.
segments.s098-g40: Again چو introduces 'when/as'; شیر اندرآید uses شیر 'lion' + اندرآید (classical present/enter = 'enters/comes in') giving a simile 'as a lion he comes in'; کند کارزار is 'he makes/does battle' (کند = does/makes, کارزار = battle). The two parts form the main clause: when the mother sees him thus, he enters the fight like a lion.

sentences.s098-s5: By his strength you bent his back and laid him down on the ground on his belly.
sentences.s098-s6: So it was until from Kabul came Zarang; Fasīleh kept rushing, multicolored.
sentences.s098-s7: A swift mare passed by — stupid/dazed; her bearing like a lion’s, short and lame.
sentences.s098-s8: His two ears were like two sharp daggers; his fore (quarters) and mane were fat, his middle emaciated.
sentences.s098-s21: Rostam the Kayanian cast the lasso; suddenly he brought up its head and bound it.
sentences.s098-s22: His mother came like a living lion; she tried to tear off his head with her teeth.
sentences.s098-s23: Rostam roared like a living lion; the mare was stunned by his voice.
sentences.s098-s24: Someone struck a blow as well upon his neck; from that blow his body recoiled, trembling.
sentences.s098-s13: He cast the royal lasso, bending it, so that it might recapture that foal from the flight.
sentences.s098-s14: To Rostam thus spoke the old shepherd: "O chief, do not seize the people's horses."
sentences.s098-s15: Rostam asked, "Whose horse is this?" for its two flanks are devoid of the heat of fire.
sentences.s098-s16: Thus he answered: “Do not look for its mark, for from that springs every sort of talk.”
sentences.s098-s1: Thus, by his words the hero became such that his speech would scatter the spirit.
sentences.s098-s2: He brought whatever herds he had in Zabulistan for a short while to Kabulistan.
sentences.s098-s3: They drove them all before Rostam, and continually sang the kings' lament.
sentences.s098-s4: Every horse that Rostam had drawn forward, he would press his hand upon its back.
sentences.s098-s25: He fell, rose, and turned away from him; then he set his face swiftly toward the herd.
sentences.s098-s26: Rostam the mighty squeezed the thigh and tightened the bend of the lasso.
sentences.s098-s27: The claws opened, throwing up dust by force; with a squeeze he pressed one hand upon the fair one's back.
sentences.s098-s28: He did not, by pressing, make the back empty at all; you said there is no news.
sentences.s098-s9: One foal followed behind her; on its back was a saddle, and its breadth was as wide as hers.
sentences.s098-s10: Black‑eyed and with a dun (brown) flank and a cow‑tail; black‑testicled, swift and iron‑hoofed.
sentences.s098-s11: Its body was richly ornamented from edge to edge, like the mark of a red flower upon saffron.
sentences.s098-s12: When Rostam looked at that mare, he saw that Piltan foal.
sentences.s098-s37: His cloud became such that every night they burned harmal for fear of harm.
sentences.s098-s38: They said, left and right, that it had been enchanted and that it had been made into a living gazelle.
sentences.s098-s39: Zal's heart turned to gold like the joyful spring at the sight of the newly‑adorned steed and the fortunate rider.
sentences.s098-s40: He opened the treasure and gave out dinars; from then on remembrance did not come to him.
sentences.s098-s33: Rostam's lip split with laughter; when it subsided he kept saying, “Goodness befits God.”
sentences.s098-s34: He put Golrang into the saddle; his head grew keen for hatred and for battle.
sentences.s098-s35: He saw a wide jaw and beheld the sharp little dagger; he saw that he possessed heart, radiance, and veins.
sentences.s098-s36: He donned his cuirass and took up his Koopal; his body was layered in mail, and his breast and mane (were so) as well.
sentences.s098-s29: Badal said, “This one who has taken the seat is mine; now the doing of the deed is in my hand.”
sentences.s098-s30: He asked the shepherd about this dragon — how large it was and what price he wanted for it.
sentences.s098-s31: He replied thus: 'If Rostam goes, let him go straight — direct his face toward the land of Iran.'
sentences.s098-s32: To me this is the value for the land and soil of Iran; by this you will set the world right.
sentences.s098-s17: We call him Rakhsh; he is his match — fiery in disposition and the color of flame.
sentences.s098-s18: Only God knows this; we always call Rakhsh Rostam's, and that's enough.
sentences.s098-s19: Three years have passed since this saddle arrived; it came before the eyes of the great and was chosen.
sentences.s098-s20: When his mother saw him girded with a lasso and mounted, like a lion he entered and made war.
